event: Jericho emerges as one of the world's earliest continuously inhabited settlements during the Neolithic period.
Pre-Pottery Neolithic communities at Jericho build a massive stone tower and defensive wall, marking early proto-urban development.
Early Bronze Age Canaanite urban centers develop across the region, with fortified towns and trade links to Egypt and Mesopotamia.
Canaanite city-states such as Hazor, Megiddo, and Lachish flourish during the Middle Bronze Age.
Egyptian pharaoh Thutmose III defeats a Canaanite coalition at the Battle of Megiddo, bringing the region under Egyptian control.
The Philistines settle along the southern coastal plain, giving rise to a pentapolis including Gaza and Ashkelon.
Israelite and Judahite kingdoms consolidate in the highlands, with Jerusalem becoming a political and religious center.
The Neo-Assyrian Empire conquers the northern Kingdom of Israel and incorporates the region into its provincial system.
The Babylonians under Nebuchadnezzar II destroy Jerusalem and its temple, ending the Kingdom of Judah.
The Persian Achaemenid Empire takes control after conquering Babylon, allowing exiled communities to return and rebuild Jerusalem.
Alexander the Great conquers the region, beginning centuries of Hellenistic rule under the Ptolemies and Seleucids.
The Roman general Pompey captures Jerusalem, bringing the region under Roman authority.
Roman forces under Titus destroy the Second Temple in Jerusalem during the suppression of the Jewish revolt.
Rome crushes the Bar Kokhba revolt and renames the province Syria Palaestina, popularizing the name Palestine.
The region becomes part of the Christian Byzantine Empire, and Jerusalem develops as a major Christian pilgrimage center.
Sasanian Persian forces capture Jerusalem during their war with the Byzantine Empire.
Muslim Arab armies under Caliph Umar take Jerusalem, beginning Islamic rule over Palestine.
The Umayyad caliph Abd al-Malik completes the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem, a landmark of early Islamic architecture.
Abbasid rule replaces the Umayyads, shifting the center of the Islamic empire eastward to Baghdad.
The Fatimid Caliphate of Egypt extends its control over Palestine.
Crusaders capture Jerusalem and establish the Latin Kingdom of Jerusalem.
Saladin defeats the Crusaders at the Battle of Hattin and recaptures Jerusalem for the Ayyubids.
The Mamluks defeat the Mongols at Ain Jalut in Palestine and establish enduring control over the region.
The Ottoman Empire conquers Palestine, beginning four centuries of Ottoman rule.
Napoleon Bonaparte invades Ottoman Palestine but fails to capture Acre and withdraws.
Egyptian forces under Muhammad Ali briefly seize Palestine before Ottoman authority is restored in 1840.
The First Aliyah brings waves of Jewish immigration to Ottoman Palestine, spurred by persecution in Europe.
British forces capture Palestine from the Ottomans, and the Balfour Declaration backs a Jewish national home there.
Britain assumes administration of Palestine, formalized as a League of Nations Mandate in 1922.
The Arab Revolt erupts against British rule and Jewish immigration, lasting until 1939.
The UN adopts Resolution 181 partitioning Palestine into Arab and Jewish states, which Arab leaders reject.
Israel declares independence and the ensuing war causes the Nakba, the displacement of about 700,000 Palestinians.
Armistice agreements leave the West Bank under Jordanian control and the Gaza Strip under Egyptian administration.
The Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) is founded to pursue Palestinian national aspirations.
Israel captures the West Bank, East Jerusalem, and the Gaza Strip in the Six-Day War, beginning the occupation.
Yasser Arafat becomes chairman of the PLO, leading it for decades as the dominant Palestinian movement.
The UN General Assembly recognizes the PLO as the representative of the Palestinian people and grants it observer status.
The First Intifada, a mass Palestinian uprising against Israeli occupation, breaks out and Hamas is founded.
The PLO declares an independent State of Palestine and recognizes Israel, accepting a two-state framework.
The Oslo Accords are signed, establishing the Palestinian Authority and a framework for limited self-rule.
The Palestinian Authority assumes governance in Gaza and Jericho as Yasser Arafat returns from exile.
The Second Intifada erupts after the collapse of peace talks, bringing years of intense violence.
Yasser Arafat dies, and Mahmoud Abbas is elected Palestinian Authority president the following year.
Israel withdraws its settlers and military from the Gaza Strip in a unilateral disengagement.
Hamas wins Palestinian legislative elections, leading to a political split with Fatah.
Hamas seizes control of the Gaza Strip, dividing Palestinian governance between Gaza and the West Bank.
The UN General Assembly upgrades Palestine to non-member observer state status.
A major war erupts between Israel and Hamas in Gaza, causing extensive casualties and destruction.
A Hamas-led attack on Israel triggers a devastating Israeli military campaign in Gaza, causing a humanitarian catastrophe.
